title: AI in Daily Life
subtitle: AI Is Already Everywhere — Can You Spot It?
overview: Artificial Intelligence is no longer science fiction — it is already woven into the fabric of everyday life. From the moment you wake up to when you go to sleep, AI is making decisions that affect you: recommending songs, filtering spam, unlocking your phone with your face, predicting traffic, and detecting diseases. In this session, students discover the AI hidden in everyday technology, understand how it works at a high level, explore its benefits and risks, and think critically about how AI shapes the world they live in.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — AI application examples, recommendation algorithm diagrams, facial recognition demo, student activity worksheets.

keyTopics: Artificial Intelligence (AI) — Computer systems that perform tasks that normally require human intelligence: recognising speech, making decisions, translating languages
Machine Learning — A type of AI where systems learn patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed with rules
Recommendation System — AI that analyses your past behaviour and others' to suggest content you are likely to enjoy (Netflix, Spotify, YouTube, Amazon)
Spam Filter — An AI classifier trained on millions of emails to distinguish legitimate messages from unwanted junk and phishing attempts
Facial Recognition — AI that detects a face in an image, extracts unique features, and matches them to a known identity; used in phone unlock and security
Voice Assistant — AI that converts speech to text, understands intent, and responds (Alexa, Siri, Google Assistant, Cortana)
Predictive Text — AI trained on vast amounts of text that suggests the next word as you type on a smartphone keyboard
Navigation AI — Google Maps and Waze use AI to predict traffic, suggest fastest routes, and update directions in real time based on live data
AI in Healthcare — AI analyses medical images (X-rays, MRIs) to detect diseases; predicts patient risk; assists diagnosis
AI Bias — When AI systems produce unfair or discriminatory outcomes because of biased training data reflecting real-world inequalities
Filter Bubble — The tendency of recommendation algorithms to show you content that reinforces your existing views, limiting exposure to different perspectives
Ethical AI — Designing and using AI responsibly: fairly, transparently, safely, and with accountability for its decisions

activity.title: AI Spot It! + Ethics Discussion + Recommendation Audit
parts: Computer or tablet with internet access
Access to YouTube or Spotify for recommendation audit
Printed scenario cards for ethics discussion
steps: Part 1 — AI Spot It!: Students map out their day from waking up to going to sleep and identify every moment AI was involved. Compare lists as a class — who found the most? Categories: transport, entertainment, communication, health, shopping, security

Part 2 — Recommendation Audit: Open YouTube or Spotify. Look at the recommended videos or songs. Ask: why is this being recommended? What does the algorithm know about you? How would the recommendations differ for someone with different tastes? Discuss the Filter Bubble effect

Part 3 — Spam Filter Experiment: Look at the spam/junk email folder. Examine 3–5 spam emails — what features do they have in common? How might an AI identify them as spam? List 5 clues (urgent language, suspicious links, unknown sender, grammar errors, requests for personal info)

Part 4 — Voice Assistant Test: Use Google Assistant, Siri, or Alexa (or a demo). Ask it 5 questions — factual, creative, local, personal, and ambiguous. Evaluate: how well did it understand intent? Where did it fail? What training data would it need?

Part 5 — AI Benefits vs Risks: Groups each take one AI application (facial recognition, recommendation algorithm, medical AI, self-driving car, AI in hiring). Create a T-chart of benefits and risks. Present to class

Part 6 — AI Bias Scenario: Read a real case of AI bias (e.g. Amazon's hiring AI discriminated against women; facial recognition misidentifies darker-skinned faces). Discuss: where did the bias come from? Who is responsible? How could it be fixed?

Part 7 — My AI Bill of Rights: Each student writes 3 rights they believe people should have regarding AI systems that affect them — e.g. right to know when AI is making a decision about you, right to appeal an AI decision, right to opt out

Challenge: Research one AI application that is changing India specifically — healthcare AI detecting tuberculosis from X-rays, AI for crop disease detection in farming, AI for traffic management in Mumbai. Present how it works and its social impact

quiz.q: Which of these everyday technologies uses Artificial Intelligence?
options: A traditional wired telephone
Netflix suggesting your next show, Google Maps predicting traffic, and your phone's face unlock
A USB flash drive storing your files
A calculator performing arithmetic
quiz.explanation: AI is everywhere in modern tech! Netflix uses AI to recommend content, Google Maps uses AI to predict traffic and find optimal routes, and face unlock uses AI-powered facial recognition. Traditional phones, USB drives, and calculators use rules-based logic — not AI learning from data.

options: A system that recommends which programming language to learn
AI that analyses your past behaviour and patterns from similar users to suggest content, products, or services you are likely to enjoy
A teacher recommendation letter generated by AI
A system that recommends hardware upgrades for your computer
quiz.explanation: Recommendation systems power the internet economy! When Netflix shows 'Because you watched...', Spotify creates Discover Weekly, or Amazon shows 'Customers also bought...' — all are AI systems finding patterns in your behaviour and millions of other users' to predict what you want next.

quiz.q: How does a Spam Filter use AI?
options: It blocks all emails from unknown senders automatically
It is trained on millions of labelled emails to learn the patterns of spam — suspicious keywords, unknown senders, urgent language — and classifies new emails as spam or legitimate
It checks each email against a government blocklist
It only filters emails containing links
quiz.explanation: Spam filters are classifiers! Trained on millions of examples of spam and legitimate email, the AI learns patterns — words like 'FREE!!!', urgent demands, suspicious links, mismatched sender addresses. When a new email arrives it predicts: spam or not spam? Getting it right 99.9% of the time, millions of times per day.

quiz.q: What is Facial Recognition AI and where is it used?
options: Software that draws cartoon faces
AI that detects a face, extracts unique facial features, and matches them to a known identity — used in phone unlock, security cameras, and border control
An app that applies filters to selfies
AI that detects emotions from facial expressions only
quiz.explanation: Facial recognition has three steps: detect (find the face in the image), extract (map unique landmarks — eye distance, nose shape, jaw line), match (compare to database). Used for phone Face ID, airport security, attendance systems, and law enforcement — with significant privacy and accuracy concerns.

quiz.q: What is a Voice Assistant and how does it understand you?
options: A human assistant who responds to voice commands remotely
AI that converts your speech to text, analyses the meaning and intent using natural language processing, and generates an appropriate response
A microphone that amplifies and records your voice
Software that searches the internet using keywords you say
quiz.explanation: Voice assistants combine multiple AI systems! Speech-to-text converts audio to words. Natural Language Processing understands what you meant (intent). A knowledge system or search generates the answer. Text-to-speech speaks the response. Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant, and Cortana all use this multi-stage AI pipeline.

quiz.q: What is the Filter Bubble effect caused by recommendation algorithms?
options: Algorithms filtering out inappropriate content from social media
The tendency of recommendation AI to keep showing content similar to what you already like, narrowing your exposure to different opinions, news, and perspectives
A privacy filter that prevents apps from accessing your data
An algorithm that removes duplicate content from your feed
quiz.explanation: Filter bubbles can polarise society! If you watch political videos from one viewpoint, the algorithm recommends more of the same — you never see opposing perspectives. Over time, your worldview is shaped by a curated narrow slice of content. Being aware of this effect is the first step to breaking out of it.

quiz.q: What is AI Bias and how does it occur?
options: AI systems that favour one brand of hardware over another
When AI produces unfair or discriminatory outcomes because it was trained on data that reflects existing human biases and inequalities
When an AI model is biased toward being too accurate
Bias that only affects AI systems in certain countries
quiz.explanation: Bias in data = bias in AI! Amazon's hiring AI was found to discriminate against women because it was trained on historical hiring data where most hires were male. Some facial recognition systems perform worse on darker skin tones because training datasets had fewer diverse faces. Garbage in, garbage out — biased data creates biased AI.

quiz.q: How does Google Maps use AI to suggest the fastest route?
options: It uses GPS satellites to directly measure road speed
It analyses historical traffic patterns, real-time speed data from millions of phones, and machine learning models to predict journey times and identify the optimal route
It checks a pre-programmed timetable of road speeds
It counts the number of traffic lights on each route
quiz.explanation: Google Maps is a massive AI system! It collects anonymous speed data from millions of phones, learns traffic patterns by time of day and day of week, detects accidents and roadworks in real time, and uses ML models to predict which route will be fastest when you arrive at each junction. It improves with every trip taken by every user.

quiz.q: What is AI in Healthcare being used for today?
options: AI performs surgical operations completely autonomously
AI analyses medical images (X-rays, MRIs, retinal scans) to detect diseases, predicts patient risk, assists diagnosis, and helps doctors make better decisions faster
AI replaces doctors for prescribing medicines
AI is only used in hospital administration, not medical diagnosis
quiz.explanation: AI is already saving lives! Google's DeepMind AI detects over 50 eye diseases from retinal scans as accurately as expert doctors. AI detects tuberculosis from chest X-rays. Cancer detection AI spots tumours that human radiologists sometimes miss. AI doesn't replace doctors — it gives them a powerful second opinion.

quiz.q: Predictive text on your smartphone keyboard is a form of AI. How does it work?
options: It searches your previous messages and copies common phrases
A language model trained on vast amounts of text learns which words most commonly follow other words, predicting the most likely next word as you type
It connects to the internet to search for popular phrases in real time
It uses autocorrect rules programmed by the keyboard developer
quiz.explanation: Predictive text is a miniature language model! Trained on billions of words of text, it learns that 'I am going to' is often followed by 'the', 'school', or 'sleep'. Modern keyboards also learn YOUR personal writing style over time. It's a smaller version of the same technology behind ChatGPT!

quiz.q: Why is it important to know when AI is making decisions that affect you?
options: It is not important — AI decisions are always correct and fair
Transparency allows you to understand, question, and challenge AI decisions that may be wrong, unfair, or based on biased data — protecting your rights and wellbeing
Knowing about AI decisions helps you score higher on tests
It is only important for adults, not students
quiz.explanation: AI transparency is a rights issue! AI makes decisions about credit scores, job applications, bail decisions, and social media feeds. If you don't know AI is involved, you can't question a wrong decision or ask for a human review. The right to know when AI affects you is a fundamental principle of ethical AI.

quiz.q: What does it mean for AI to be used 'ethically'?
options: Using AI only for tasks involving ethics and philosophy
Designing and deploying AI that is fair, transparent, safe, accountable, and respects human rights — avoiding discrimination, protecting privacy, and allowing human oversight
Making AI systems as efficient as possible to reduce costs
Ensuring AI is only used by qualified engineers
quiz.explanation: Ethical AI is everyone's responsibility! It means: fairness (no discrimination), transparency (explain decisions), safety (prevent harm), privacy (protect personal data), and accountability (humans responsible for AI outcomes). Governments, companies, and individuals all have a role in ensuring AI is used for good.

quiz.q: A student notices that their YouTube feed only shows videos supporting one political view. What AI phenomenon is this an example of?
options: AI censorship — the algorithm is blocking other viewpoints
The Filter Bubble — the recommendation algorithm keeps showing similar content, narrowing the student's exposure to diverse perspectives
AI bias — the algorithm dislikes certain political views
Overfitting — the algorithm has memorised the student's preferences too precisely
quiz.explanation: Classic Filter Bubble! YouTube's algorithm optimises for watch time — it shows you more of what you already watch because you're likely to watch it again. Over time this creates an echo chamber. Solution: actively search for different viewpoints, clear your watch history, or use incognito mode to get unbiased recommendations.

quiz.q: What is the difference between traditional programming and Machine Learning?
options: Traditional programming uses computers; machine learning uses robots
Traditional programming requires humans to write explicit rules; machine learning systems learn rules automatically from patterns in data
Machine learning only works for images; traditional programming handles all other tasks
Traditional programming is faster; machine learning is more accurate
quiz.explanation: This is the fundamental shift! Traditional: programmer writes rule 'if email contains FREE!!! → spam'. Machine Learning: show the system 1 million spam emails and 1 million legitimate emails, and it figures out the rules itself — often finding patterns no human programmer would think to code. Data replaces explicit rules.

quiz.q: A company's AI hiring tool rejects more female applicants than male ones for engineering roles. What is the most likely cause?
options: The AI was deliberately programmed to discriminate against women
The AI was trained on historical hiring data where most successful engineering hires were male — it learned to associate maleness with job success, reflecting past human bias
Women's CVs use different fonts that the AI cannot read
The AI randomly selects candidates and the result is a coincidence
quiz.explanation: This actually happened at Amazon! Their AI hiring tool was trained on 10 years of hiring data — from a time when the tech industry hired far more men. The AI learned 'male = good candidate' from the data. Biased historical data created biased AI. The fix: audit training data for representation and regularly test AI systems for discriminatory outcomes.
